ARTEMIS · five-county north-Florida pilot

From a validated baseline to management-unit harvest scheduling

A spatially-explicit forest-projection pipeline. The no-management growth baseline runs end-to-end today; the current build extends it into constrained, per-unit harvest simulation.

693 stands 5.41M forested pixels FVS Southern variant ~50-year horizon · 30 m · EPSG:5070 meeting brief 2026-07-23

Use ← → or space to move through the deck.

The pipeline 01

One pipeline, three stages

The growth baseline is built and running. The management-unit and scheduling stack is the work in progress; managed re-projection is next.

Baseline — working today

1 · Initial state

TreeMap 2022 + FIA tree lists → 693 FVS-ready stands.

2 · FVS growth

Southern variant, 5-year cycles, no management.

3 · Paint to pixels

Stand trajectories → 5.41M TreeMap pixels.

Toward management units

4 · Management-unit map

Parcels ∩ forest − road/water/BMP, then sliver-merge.

5 · TPO constraints

Volume caps by county & ownership (~72M cuft/yr).

6 · Harvest scheduler

Global barriers, selective per-stand cut.

Managed scenarios — next

7 · Managed re-projection

Re-run FVS under the schedule; compare managed vs. baseline by county, owner, forest type.

Working today In progress this cycle Designed / next

Alternative delineation 04

We tested smarter delineation — segmentation

Finding: with default parameters, segmentation does not reduce fragmentation — every method is 87–90% slivers. It's the BMP erase that shatters polygons, not the delineation method.

StrategyUnitsSliver %Median haCompactness
Naive (parcel ∩ forest)17,02087.30.100.46
Felzenszwalb21,21090.40.090.53
SLIC11,72187.10.100.50

Why 05

The size distributions barely move

The area curves sit on top of each other and the size-class bars are overwhelmingly slivers. Segmentation buys a modest compactness gain (rounder units) — but not a usable size distribution.

Delineation + merge 06

Add the merge — and they all converge

StrategyRaw → mergedSliver %Median haCompactness
Naive17,020 → 2,4420.06.80.20
Felzenszwalb21,210 → 2,1370.07.40.16
SLIC11,721 → 1,5540.08.70.13
Takeaway: the sliver-merge is the load-bearing step; delineation is a second-order knob. The trade-off it exposes: merging lowers compactness (the nearest-unit fallback makes multipart units) — naive+merge stays most compact.

FVS engine fidelity 07

The engine for managed runs is proven

The scheduler must pause FVS at a 5-year barrier, cut the stands it selects, and resume — and FVS must respond identically to a run with the cut written in from the start. Measured, not assumed.

10 / 11
checks PASS on in-scope metrics
0.0
max Δ on BA/TPA/SDI, every restart
30.0%
removed by a 30% thin — to the digit
5 / 5
stands round-tripped through a barrier
CheckMetricObservedResult
Stop/restart preserves stand valuesBA/TPA/SDI Δ0.0PASS
Multi-stand global barrier (5 stands)25 rows Δ0.0PASS
Runtime cut = native FVS keywordall metrics Δ0.0PASS
Selective cut — target stand onlyTPA post/pre0.700PASS
FFE carbon under restart OUT OF SCOPEtotal carbon−8.3%FAIL
The one FAIL is carbon, deliberately parked. Stop/restart understates one FFE carbon pool ~8% while stand values stay bit-identical. Carbon is out of scope for v1 (guarded by a tripwire test). Fixtures are narrow — 6 stands, one variant, no fire.

Terms — methods & shape metrics 08

How to explain the delineation figures

Felzenszwalb segmentation

Grows patches by gluing together neighboring pixels that look alike, following the natural edges in the vegetation raster. One dial (scale) sets how big the patches get. Regions come out irregular and data-driven.

Say: "It groups similar neighboring pixels into blobs that hug the real edges in the map."

SLIC superpixels

You ask for ~N patches (we used 1,000) and a compactness weight. It seeds an even grid and grows each seed by balancing "similar values" against "stay close," giving compact, uniform tiles that still snap to edges.

Say: "It lays a quilt of ~1,000 evenly-sized tiles over the forest that still follow real edges."

ECDF chart

Empirical Cumulative Distribution Function. At any size on the x-axis, the curve height is the fraction of units that size or smaller — a way to compare whole distributions without choosing bins.

Say: "It's the share of units at or below a given size; a near-vertical rise at the far left means almost everything is tiny."

Polsby-Popper shape metric

A roundness score = 4π·Area ÷ Perimeter², from 0 to 1. A perfect circle = 1; a stringy or ragged shape → near 0. It's the same metric used to flag gerrymandered voting districts.

Say: "A 0-to-1 roundness score — 1 is a circle, near 0 is a sliver; the same math used to catch gerrymandered districts."

Terms — pipeline & stand metrics 09

The words behind the maps & tables

Sliver & sliver-merge

A sliver is a leftover polygon below the minimum workable stand size (5 acres ≈ 2 ha) — you can't simulate a 0.1-ha scrap. Sliver-merge dissolves each into its best neighbor (longest shared border, else nearest), conserving area with no gaps.

Naive delineation & BMP erase

Naive = a plain overlay: parcels, keep the forested part, cut out roads/water/protected strips. BMP = Best Management Practices, the required no-harvest buffers around streams & roads (35–75 ft in FL). "Erase" cuts those strips out — and that's what creates the slivers.

Management unit & "state-zero"

A management unit is one contiguous stand the model treats as a single decision area. State-zero is the clean starting map at year 0, before any simulated harvest. Size classes on the maps: red = sliver (<2 ha), green = unit (2–40 ha), amber = large (>40 ha).

Stand metrics & EPSG:5070

BA basal area (trunk cross-section per acre, a stocking measure); TPA trees per acre; SDI stand density index; QMD average trunk diameter. EPSG:5070 is the equal-area map projection — so acres/hectares measure correctly.

Where this leaves us 10

The direction, in one slide

  • Baseline works — a real spatially-explicit 50-year forest projection across five counties.
  • Units are clean — the sliver-merge turns fragmented drafts into ~2,400 runnable state-zero stands.
  • The scheduler's engine is proven — pause, selectively cut, resume, all exact against native FVS.
  • Segmentation is a tuning knob, not the fix — the merge is load-bearing; params still to tune.
  • Next — segmentation→merge tuning, run the constrained scheduler, then managed re-projection vs. baseline.
Reproducibility note: every figure in this deck was regenerated live this session against the Cloudflare R2 replica of the /mnt/d data drive — the data-availability barrier is effectively gone, which makes CI-style regeneration feasible.
